verifier.py uses 6.0 as the lower bound for C3 validation
verifier.py uses 20.0 as the upper bound for C3 validation

privileged_info_summary: The task relies on synthetically defined requirements generated by setup_task.sh. All metadata ranges (such as C3 limits of 6-20 km^2/s^2) are explicitly encoded into the verifier.py logic and accurately reported. However, an independent orbital mechanics audit via Python (using Astropy and Poliastro's Lambert solver) reveals a fatal physical contradiction: while the task instructs the agent to launch on July 14, 2026, the Earth-Mars launch window actually occurs in late October/November 2026. A transfer departing on July 14, 2026 requires a physically unavoidable minimum C3 of ~85 km^2/s^2 (TMI Delta-V > 6.5 km/s). It is impossible to achieve the required 6-20 km^2/s^2 C3 range for the given date. The task author likely copied a 2020 launch window scenario (where C3 is ~15 km^2/s^2 on July 14, 2020) and blindly updated the year to 2026. An agent correctly solving the problem with GMAT for the instructed date will perpetually fail the verifier's strict, hardcoded orbital energy bounds.
